speaker: interviewer
text: --with Sally Huppert, tape number four. When you came to this country, did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I work on [INAUDIBLE] Avenue. I work at the store on girdles and brassieres. For $35 a-- a week. This-- this was a big pension.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you stay working there or did you work elsewhere?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I left there and I worked-- went to Bergdorf Goodman in-- on Fifth Avenue. And I stayed there 20 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do at Bergdorf's? Girdles and brassieres. I make a tie. I work for-- for Mrs. Kennedy and-- he-- his mother-- Kennedy's mother, Rose, and [? Ethel-- ?] everybody. They were happy to have me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't want to leave there. They didn't pay much. But I liked the place. I liked actresses and it was very fancy.

speaker: interviewer
text: I still have 30% off, if I buy something. But who buys something? It's too expensive. And why did you leave Bergdorf's?

speaker: interviewer
text: You retired from there. OK. And your husband? My husband couldn't get a job. He was very-- he went-- he was a chemist. It was very hard for him to get a job. They tell him he's too old. He was 42 years old. In-- he went to Mighty Fine. They say we have young students. He couldn't get a job so he bought a laundry. And he work very hard. This killed him. And when did he die?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter is all right, thank God. She's married. She has three children. She's very happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And looking back, what do you think sustained you throughout the war? What made you want to keep going?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't think. I wanted a home. I wanted family, always. We didn't have nobody. I didn't know what kind of world it's gonna be.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything about your experiences that we haven't talked about that you'd like to talk about?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm thinking. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] I-- I can't think enough for what they do to my family. I must tell you, I never, never go to parties. Never. When I must, I always think about my family, my mother, my sisters. It hurts.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any message that you'd like to leave for future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] What kind of message? [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Just [INAUDIBLE] kill-- anti-Semitism kills everything. What we can do?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was much easier with you. I'm happy you did it. I wish you luck.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the same to you. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is from when I went to start-- testified in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where-- who did you testify against, and-- and where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Against Rosenberg-- Rosenblat-- Rosenbaum, the SS murderer. He kill my-- my mother. I don't know if he killed. But he make kill them, kill my mother and my sister. In the whole-- whole grave-- [INAUDIBLE] grave was there. I was there two years ago, [SIGHS] on the grave.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went to Hamburg to testify?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I went to Hamburg, testified.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know what his sentence was?

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have-- this is from what newspaper?

speaker: interviewer
text: And the date was August 16, 1968, was the article?

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have other newspapers as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. German newspapers. German women-- I met German women. They were very nice. And they tried to write me and send me the pictures-- the-- from the paper. The clips.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Oh. And what is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture from my cousin's wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: And approximately when and where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Cracow, in my aunt's house.

speaker: interviewer
text: About what year? How old were you in that picture?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you say you were about 20?

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is-- who was the bride? What was her name?

speaker: interviewer
text: And is your mother in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She's next to my grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where-- where are they in the picture? Are they on-- on the-- the right or the left?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. The woman on the far left in the front row is your--

speaker: interviewer
text: And where are you in-- are you in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I am on the right side. The second from the right.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me on-- right after the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is taken in Cracow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister, Bankier, Abraham Bankier, my brother-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister, what-- which sister is that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know who the other man is?

speaker: interviewee
text: The other man is a brother-law.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my daughter. She-- she was born in Konstanz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Mary Ann. Mary Ann Huppert.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, that's when she was born. It was probably taken a little after that. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is my husband and me. My husband, Max Huppert, and Sally Huppert, with the baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken when we came out on the boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: No? And when was that? When did you arrive in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is Abraham Bankier and my sister [INAUDIBLE] Rega Bankier, and their-- and their daughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my sister, Rega Bankier.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when and where was this taken? This was taken-- Cracow, I think. Does she still live in Cracow?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And the back of it says it was taken in '81.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is my daughter, Mary Ann, in-- Mary Ann Lisnitzer and her husband, Ivan Lisnitzer. And David is one son, and-- and the girls are twins.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- can you tell us which one's which?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Robyn's the first one. Malinda's is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Robyn is the one on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: And Malinda is on the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [INAUDIBLE] Mrs. Huppert, could you introduce the people who have joined you?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my daughter and my son-in-law and my children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you give their names, please?

speaker: interviewer
text: And Mary Ann, did your mom talk about her experiences when you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She would never verbalize anything she had gone through. She would just say, you must never forget, it was terrible, and I'm glad you don't have to live through it. But I never knew of her actual experiences.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how do you feel about her doing this tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it's wonderful. I mean, people will only remember if they have something to listen to, or someone to listen to. Once you have a description of what an individual has to go through, I think you have a more emotional feeling and reaction to that. So I think it's a good move for her, and maybe I could watch it and learn some things that I haven't been told about so far. And I think it's good to pass down all of this to my children, and then perhaps to their children, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you. Is there anything anyone else would like to say? Or-- no?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd like to say that I'm very proud and pleased that she's here with us today and making these statements for the future. It's something I wanted her to do for a long time. And I'm-- I know how painful it must have been for her. But we're proud that she's here and had the family she's had, and that we have the opportunity to look at what she said and pass it down to our children and others, so they don't forget.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody should forget this, what I went through. Every day I was afraid I gonna be killed. In Auschwitz, we-- we say, goodbye, I see you on the-- on the soap-- on-- soap-- where the soap, they put-- they put the-- they make the soap of the fat of the people.
